aliases: Fragrant Basmati Rice with Dried Fruits

english: Fragrant Basmati Rice with Dried Fruits

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kashmiri Rice; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03301710) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kashmiri Rice; A000E (rice-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kashmiri Rice as a specific food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kashmiri Rice is approximately 200 g (about 1 cup cooked), as commonly plated at a Singaporean Indian restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Kashmiri Rice — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Kashmiri Rice (cr_id G00010685). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kashmiri Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 57 based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked basmati rice (GI ~57, medium category), which is the primary carbohydrate component. The dried fruits and apple contribute additional sugars that may modestly elevate the effective glycaemic load; however, the fibre content (3.7 g/100g) provides partial mitigation. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (57 × 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2) / 100 = 28.8 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline elevated, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100g (favourable K:Na ratio of ~1.4:1 per DASH criteria)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced or no added salt during preparation and limit portion size to 150 g to keep sodium intake below 350 mg per serving; the high potassium content from dried fruits and nuts provides meaningful DASH-diet benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Basmati rice GI ~57 (medium) combined with concentrated sugars from dried fruits raises glycaemic load to ~28.8 per 200 g serving, exceeding the low-GL threshold of 20
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–120 g, request reduced dried fruit content to lower the glycaemic load, and pair with a high-protein or high-fibre accompaniment to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~1.5 g/100g (from ghee or oil used in preparation); cholesterol is 0 mg/100g; potassium and fibre are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should request oil substitution for ghee to reduce saturated fat, keep portions to 150–200 g, and benefit from the potassium (327 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) which support blood pressure and lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (saffron, cinnamon, cardamom) and nuts (vitamin E, MUFAs); low saturated fat; fibre supports gut-immune axis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kashmiri Rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when prepared with oil rather than ghee and with moderate dried fruit quantities to avoid excess sugar-driven inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kashmiri Rice is a fragrant Indian rice dish originating from the Kashmir region, widely served in Singaporean Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It is prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice with dried fruits such as raisins and apricots, fresh apple pieces, and a blend of aromatic spices including saffron, cardamom, and cinnamon, typically finished with ghee or oil and garnished with toasted nuts such as almonds, cashews, and pistachios. The result is a mildly sweet, subtly spiced, and aromatic rice with a soft, fluffy texture and a golden-yellow hue from saffron. Per 100 g, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrates, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The potassium content of 327 mg/100g and fibre level are cardioprotective and DASH-diet-friendly. However, the moderate glycaemic index (~57) of basmati rice combined with concentrated sugars from dried fruits produces a high glycaemic load per serving (~28.8 GL per 200 g), making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (saffron, cinnamon, cardamom) and nut content (vitamin E, MUFAs) make this dish relatively favourable for inflammatory conditions when prepared with oil rather than ghee. It is gluten-free and suitable for vegetarians and halal diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical entry for Kashmiri Rice (cr_id G00010685) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean food records. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate component, with the estimation basis clearly documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Kashmiri Rice (G00010685) and are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.5 g/100g based on typical ghee/oil content in Kashmiri Rice preparations per ICMR-NIN reference data for similar rice-based dishes; this is an approximation and should be verified if a direct HPB saturated fat value becomes available. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source. 4. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kashmiri Rice as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. Kashmiri Rice is classified as Indian cuisine with Singaporean regional relevance (HPB Singapore source); multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
